stories.headline: Denver and Colorado grapple with renaming sites honoring César Chávez after abuse allegations surface
stories.summary:
Denver city leaders and community groups are reassessing the legacy of César Chávez following a New York Times investigation that detailed allegations of sexual abuse, including a rape allegation from fellow labor leader Dolores Huerta, now 95, and accounts from two other women who said Chávez molested them beginning when they were 12 or 13 years old. The César Chávez Peace and Justice Committee of Denver announced it is canceling its annual April 11 march and celebration, which typically steps off from Regis University and ends at César Chávez Park.

Denver City Councilwoman Jamie Torres, who has sponsored annual proclamations honoring Chávez, called for renaming both the city holiday observed on March 30 and César Chávez Park in northwest Denver. Council President Amanda Sandoval said she planned to meet with Mayor Mike Johnston's office about the renaming process.

Mayor Johnston's spokesman Jon Ewing said the city is 'appalled by the allegations' and is collecting information to evaluate next steps. Denver has recognized César Chávez Day as a city holiday since 2002, renamed Alcott Park in his honor in 2005, and installed a statue of the activist in 2015. The United Farm Workers union, which Chávez founded, had already begun distancing itself from upcoming celebrations before the Times report was published.

stories.headline: RTD faces $215 million deficit, may cut bus and train service across metro Denver
stories.summary:
Regional Transportation District managers told elected directors Tuesday that the agency must close a $215 million budget gap by 2027, raising the prospect of significant reductions or eliminations of bus and train service across RTD's 2,345-square-mile service area spanning eight counties. RTD Chief Financial Officer Kelly Mackey described the situation as a 'viability issue,' warning that credit ratings agencies have demanded a balanced budget by 2027 to avoid a downgrade from the current S&P Global rating of AA+.

RTD General Manager and CEO Debra Johnson declined to specify which services might be cut, calling it premature, while saying the agency is exploring a reorganization of operations to improve efficiency. Directors reacted with frustration, initially refusing to enter a closed executive session where legal advice on possible job cuts was to be provided.

The financial crisis comes at a difficult moment for RTD, which recently approved a record $1.5 billion budget and has seen ridership lag even as state lawmakers push transit-oriented development as a solution to housing affordability and air pollution. State legislators are also drafting legislation to overhaul RTD's governance by replacing some of its 15 elected directors with appointees.

stories.headline: Update: Man arrested on first-degree murder charge in northeast Denver shooting
stories.summary:
Denver police announced Wednesday evening the arrest of Lavon Wedgeworth, 44, on suspicion of first-degree murder in connection with a fatal shooting that occurred Tuesday in the 12400 block of East 42nd Avenue, near the Interstate 70 exit at Peoria Street. Officers found an adult male suffering from an apparent gunshot wound and transported him to an area hospital, where he was pronounced dead.

Wedgeworth's arrest came roughly 30 hours after the shooting was reported. Final determination of charges will be made by the Denver District Attorney's Office.

The northeast Denver homicide is one of several violent incidents under investigation by Denver police this week. The arrest marks a quick resolution to what had been an open homicide case.

stories.headline: Denver-based Crusoe to open $200 million modular data center factory in Brighton, creating 200-plus jobs
stories.summary:
Denver-based artificial intelligence infrastructure company Crusoe announced it is leasing and building out a 352,000-square-foot factory in Brighton to manufacture modular, prefabricated data centers it calls Crusoe Spark. The company's investment in the facility and an initial fleet of modular units totals roughly $200 million, with production expected to begin in the second half of 2026.

The Brighton factory, dubbed Spark Factory, is expected to create more than 200 jobs and add to Crusoe's existing workforce of more than 500 employees in Colorado, Oklahoma and Louisiana. Crusoe also announced a new cloud product, Crusoe Edge Zones, which enables low-latency artificial intelligence inference computing at locations served by the modular units.

Crusoe co-founders Cully Cavness and Chase Lochmiller, who met as classmates at Kent Denver School, started the company in 2018 originally to power cryptocurrency mining using otherwise-wasted methane at oil and gas sites. The company has since pivoted to AI infrastructure and is one of two Colorado 'decacorns' — private companies valued at $10 billion or more. Governor Jared Polis praised the announcement, calling Colorado 'the best place in the country to build the future.'

stories.headline: DIA general counsel sues city, alleges retaliation over Park Hill Golf Course land deal and Key Lime Air lease
stories.summary:
Denver International Airport Executive Vice President and General Counsel Everett Ben Martinez filed a lawsuit against the city and three of Mayor Mike Johnston's top political appointees, alleging he was retaliated against for raising concerns about the city's Park Hill Golf Course land swap and a rejected airport lease with Key Lime Air. The suit names three senior Johnston administration officials.

Martinez alleges he faced professional repercussions after spotlighting what he considered improper dealings involving the Park Hill Golf Course land transaction and the city's decision to vote down an airport lease agreement with Key Lime Air, a regional airline that has drawn protests. The specific nature of the alleged retaliation was not detailed in the limited publicly available text of the filing.

The lawsuit adds to ongoing scrutiny of the Johnston administration's handling of DIA affairs and the Park Hill Golf Course redevelopment, which has been a source of political controversy in Denver. Key Lime Air has previously been a subject of community opposition at DIA.

stories.headline: Xcel Energy substation failure causes one-hour DIA power outage, grounding flights and stranding travelers
stories.summary:
A failure at an Xcel Energy substation caused a roughly one-hour power outage at Denver International Airport on Wednesday morning, prompting the Federal Aviation Administration to issue a ground stop for flights headed to DIA from 9:54 a.m. to 11:30 a.m. The outage froze escalators, security screening conveyor belts and the underground train system connecting DIA's main terminal to its concourses, leaving travelers backed up on train platforms and at TSA checkpoints.

Airport CEO Phil Washington confirmed that 'critical airport systems remained without power for roughly an hour,' with power restored around 11 a.m. and full operations resuming by noon. Xcel Energy spokeswoman Michelle Aguayo said crews had been powering up a new transformer at one of two substations serving DIA when it lost power; the second substation was unaffected and was able to partially serve the airport during the outage.

Flight delays affected travelers connecting through or arriving from Albuquerque, Chicago, Los Angeles, Salt Lake City, Houston, Oakland, Cleveland, Dallas-Fort Worth, Kansas City, Seattle, Indianapolis, Memphis and Minneapolis. The incident renewed interest among DIA officials in building an on-site mini-power plant; Washington said the airport 'continues to explore alternative energy solutions via a request for information from the private sector' to ensure greater redundancy.

stories.headline: Colorado lawmakers consider alcohol and marijuana tax increases to fund new Aurora mental health facility
stories.summary:
Colorado Democratic lawmakers have introduced legislation that would raise taxes and fees on alcohol and marijuana by tens of millions of dollars annually to help address the state's $850 million-plus budget shortfall and fund new mental health infrastructure. House Bill 26-1301 would generate approximately $63 million per year and direct the funds toward construction and operation of a 50-to-70-bed Mental Health Institute in Aurora for people committed to state institutions or ordered into substance use treatment; the measure would require voter approval if it passes the legislature.

A separate measure, House Bill 26-1271, would impose fees on beer, wine and spirits totaling roughly $30 million annually, with proceeds flowing into three enterprises funding alcohol prevention, treatment and recovery programs. Brewery, winery and distillery owners testified against both bills, arguing that Colorado's alcohol industry already pays $60 million a year in excise taxes and has lost hundreds of businesses over the past five years.

The House Health and Human Services Committee killed House Bill 26-1271 amid concerns about job losses in the hospitality and craft beverage industry. The fate of House Bill 26-1301 remained pending as of Wednesday.

stories.headline: Colorado AI task force unveils third attempt at rewriting state's artificial intelligence regulations
stories.summary:
A task force of consumer advocates and technology groups released a proposed framework Tuesday to rewrite Colorado's artificial intelligence regulations, the third attempt in two years to find legislative consensus on governing the rapidly growing technology. Under the proposal, courts would determine liability between AI developers and organizations using AI tools on a case-by-case basis when discrimination results from the use of AI in 'consequential decisions' such as those affecting employment, housing, financial services, health care and government services.

Governor Jared Polis praised the unanimous agreement reached by the Colorado AI Policy Working Group and said he looks forward to supporting the framework as legislation. Without any action this spring, Colorado's original first-in-the-nation AI regulations, signed in 2024, would take effect in June as originally written; lawmakers voted in August to delay the start date to allow another revision attempt.

The framework must now be translated into legislation and navigate the full legislative process, the same dynamic that derailed two prior reform attempts during the 2025 regular session and a subsequent special session. Consumer tools including spell-check, calculators, spreadsheets, robocall filters and large language models such as ChatGPT would not be covered under the proposed rules.

stories.headline: Buttermilk wildfire forces evacuations north of Westcliffe in Custer County
stories.summary:
A wildfire burning on approximately 58 acres in southern Colorado forced evacuations for residents living north of Westcliffe in Custer County on Wednesday, with the Custer County Sheriff's Office sending notices to people within a two-mile radius and deputies going door to door. The fire, initially called the 299 fire and later renamed the Buttermilk fire, was reported at 2:38 p.m. burning off County Road 179 near Buttermilk Creek, roughly nine miles northwest of Westcliffe.

A state multi-mission aircraft and helicopter assisted ground crews in fighting the blaze, according to the Colorado Division of Fire Prevention and Control. No structures had been destroyed as of Wednesday night, the sheriff's office said, noting that many of the homes in the evacuation zone are vacation properties.

The fire is the latest in a string of early-season wildfire activity across Colorado, occurring against a backdrop of abnormally warm temperatures and low snowpack that state officials have flagged as drought concerns.

stories.headline: EPA orders Colorado to rewrite six oil and gas air pollution permits over inadequate venting monitoring
stories.summary:
The U.S. Environmental Protection Agency has rejected six oil and gas air pollution permits issued by Colorado regulators, finding that the state failed to require adequate monitoring of natural gas venting at gathering systems in Garfield County. Five of the returned permits involve operations by Bargath, a subsidiary of Williams Companies; the EPA did not accept environmental groups' objections to flaring requirements in the same permits.

The Center for Biological Diversity, which brought the original objections, called the rare Trump administration rejection a significant victory and said inadequate venting monitoring is a statewide problem that allows excessive releases of volatile organic compounds and methane. The group argued that current state practices require monitoring of flaring effectiveness only once every five years.

Colorado's Air Pollution Control Division said it is reviewing the EPA's decisions and declined further comment. Environmental advocates said the issue has direct implications for the nine Front Range counties in 'severe' nonattainment of federal ozone standards, where flaring and venting are common pollution control methods at oil and gas facilities.

stories.headline: Avalanche host division-rival Dallas Stars in high-stakes Wednesday showdown at Ball Arena
stories.summary:
The Colorado Avalanche (44-13-9) hosted the Dallas Stars (41-15-10) Wednesday night at Ball Arena in a nationally televised matchup on TNT, with puck drop set for 7:50 p.m. The game carries significant Central Division implications as the two best teams in the NHL this season meet for the second time in two weeks following Colorado's 5-4 comeback win in Dallas on March 6.

New Stars acquisitions Tyler Myers and Michael Bunting, who witnessed that March 6 game from the stands before joining their new team in time to play, were both in the lineup. Colorado iced a forward group featuring the line of Valeri Nichushkin, Nathan MacKinnon and Martin Necas, with Brock Nelson and Nicolas Roy among the centers.

Dallas has eliminated Colorado from the Stanley Cup Playoffs three times this decade, including a seven-game second-round series last spring in which Mikko Rantanen — now with Dallas — scored a third-period hat trick against his former club. The rivalry has intensified as both teams have contended for the top spot in the NHL standings throughout the 2025-26 season.

stories.headline: Denver's first licensed microdosing cafe opens on South Broadway, offering psilocybin-assisted yoga and art classes
stories.summary:
Vivid Minds Cafe, located at 1545 S. Broadway, has become Colorado's first licensed microdosing cafe, offering adults 21 and older legal access to psilocybin mushrooms in small doses during group experiential sessions including yoga, sound baths and art therapy. Co-owners Manon and Jeff Parton hosted the business's first psychedelic session earlier this month and plan to expand its schedule of group classes that can be enhanced with a microdose of psilocybin served in capsules, tea or on its own.

During daytime hours, Vivid Minds operates as a standard coffee shop open to all ages, serving espresso drinks and beverages featuring non-psychedelic functional mushrooms such as lion's mane and cordyceps. Microdosing sessions, limited to groups of up to eight people, take place in a lower level of the cafe outside normal business hours; participants must be 21 or older and register in advance.

Vivid Minds joins a nascent healing center industry established after Colorado voters legalized psilocybin for therapeutic purposes in 2022. The owners said their goal is to provide a more accessible and affordable entry point into psilocybin experiences than the large-dose guided trips offered by most healing centers, while also serving as a community education hub for psychedelic-curious residents.

weather.notable: About 29 degrees above the average high for this time of year!

joke.joke: I before E except after C. We weird foreign neighbors leisurely seize neither combative deity.

topStory.headline: Israel and Iran Trade Strikes on Gas Facilities, Missile Attacks as Conflict Enters Third Week
topStory.summary:
Israel struck Iranian natural gas facilities linked to the offshore South Pars field as part of ongoing air operations, while Iran launched retaliatory cluster missile attacks against central Israel overnight Tuesday into Wednesday. The Iranian strikes killed two people — a couple in their 70s — in the Ramat Gan area near Tel Aviv, with falling shrapnel injuring additional civilians and causing property damage including at a Tel Aviv train station. Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps said the strikes were carried out as revenge for Israel's assassination of senior security official Ali Larijani.

Iran followed the overnight attack with a second wave of missile strikes on central Israel on Wednesday, even as it faced continued US and Israeli air operations on its territory. Israeli and US strikes hit multiple sites inside Iran, including a judiciary facility in the southern city of Larestan, killing and injuring civilians and judicial staff. Other strikes killed seven people in the city of Dorud and four more in the rural Chegeni region, according to Iran's Tasnim news agency.

The conflict has now entered its third week following what sources describe as a US and Israeli campaign against Iran. Among the highest-profile casualties is Ali Larijani, Iran's security chief, whose assassination Israel carried out and whose funeral was held in Tehran on Wednesday alongside that of Gholamreza Soleimani, head of Iran's Basij paramilitary force. Israeli Defense Minister Israel Katz also claimed that overnight strikes killed Iran's intelligence minister, Esmaeil Khatib, though Tehran had not confirmed the report as of Wednesday.

The International Atomic Energy Agency said Iran informed it that a projectile struck the premises of the Bushehr Nuclear Power Plant on Tuesday, though the agency reported no damage to the facility and no injuries. Iran executed a man accused of spying for Israel's Mossad intelligence agency amid what Iranian authorities described as a crackdown on alleged collaborators. The strikes on South Pars, one of the world's largest natural gas fields, rattled global energy markets.

US President Donald Trump escalated the rhetoric further by threatening to destroy Iran's South Pars gas field if Iran retaliated against Qatar following Israel's attack on the site. Pentagon Secretary Pete Hegseth held a press briefing Wednesday at a moment the department described as significant escalation, though access to the briefing was restricted under new press conditions Hegseth implemented earlier this year that several major news outlets had declined to accept.
